title: Binary Tree Level Order Traversal
problem_statement: Return the level order traversal of a binary tree’s node values.

examples.explanation: Nodes are grouped by distance from the root.

constraints: The number of nodes is in the range [0, 2000]
intuition: Use BFS to visit nodes level by level and collect values in nested lists. DFS can also work if you track depth explicitly.

approaches.name: Breadth-first search
approaches.description: Use a queue to process each level and collect values from all nodes currently in the queue before moving to the next level.
approaches.why_not: Recursive DFS can also produce level order, but it requires extra depth tracking and list management.
interview_commentary: Queue-based BFS naturally groups nodes by depth.
Use the current queue size to separate levels.
Each layer represents one additional distance from the root.

approaches.name: Depth-first search with depth tracking
approaches.description: Use recursion and the current depth to append node values into the correct nested list for each level.
approaches.why_not: DFS is less intuitive for level grouping and can use more call stack space in deep trees.
interview_commentary: Use depth to index into the result list for each level.
DFS can produce level order output with additional state.
Add a new inner list when you first reach a deeper level.

interview_qa.q: What data structure is ideal for level order traversal?
interview_qa.a: A queue, because it naturally processes nodes in first-in first-out order by depth.

interview_qa.q: How do you decide when to finish one level?
interview_qa.a: By using the queue size at the start of the level as the number of nodes to process.
It visits nodes in increasing depth order, which directly matches the required grouping.

interview_qa.q: When would DFS be useful instead?
interview_qa.a: DFS is useful when you need to build the list recursively and can carry the depth as extra state.
